subUnits.title: 7.1 Classification, Carriers, Beneficial and Harmful Effects, Growth, and Control of Microorganisms
notes: Microorganisms are ubiquitous, playing crucial roles in ecosystems, human health, agriculture, and industry. Understanding their biology is essential for harnessing benefits and controlling harms.
• Definition: Microscopic organisms, including bacteria, viruses, fungi, protozoa, and algae
b) Harmful: Salmonella (food poisoning), Mycobacterium tuberculosis (TB)
• Nigerian context: Soil bacteria essential for fertility; pathogenic bacteria cause diseases
a) Beneficial: Bacteriophages control bacterial populations
b) Harmful: Influenza virus, HIV, Cassava mosaic virus
• Nigerian context: Viral diseases affect humans, crops, livestock
• Characteristics: Eukaryotic, heterotrophic, cell walls of chitin
• Nigerian context: Fungi used in traditional fermentation; cause crop diseases
a) Beneficial: Soil protozoa contribute to nutrient cycling
• Nigerian context: Protozoan parasites cause major human and animal diseases
a) Beneficial: Phytoplankton produce oxygen, base of aquatic food webs
b) Harmful: Algal blooms deplete oxygen, produce toxins
• Nigerian context: Algae in Nigerian water bodies support fisheries; blooms can cause problems
• Definition: Organisms that transmit pathogens from one host to another
• Pathogen develops or multiplies within vector
a) Mosquitoes: Transmit malaria (Plasmodium), dengue, yellow fever
- Nigerian context: Malaria is endemic; mosquito control critical
b) Tsetse flies: Transmit sleeping sickness (Trypanosoma)
- Nigerian context: Affects humans and livestock in tsetse belts
- Nigerian context: Present in some regions
• Pathogen carried externally on vector's body
a) Houseflies: Carry bacteria on legs, mouthparts
- Transmit diarrheal diseases by contaminating food
- Nigerian context: Poor sanitation increases fly-borne disease risk
b) Cockroaches: Carry pathogens on body surfaces
- Nigerian context: Common in urban areas with poor waste management
• Gut microbiota: Aid digestion, produce vitamins, protect against pathogens
• Probiotics: Beneficial bacteria in fermented foods
• Nigerian context: Traditional fermented foods (ogi, kunun) contain beneficial microbes
• Nitrogen fixation: Rhizobium in legume root nodules
• Nigerian context: Microbial inoculants improve soil fertility in Nigerian farms
• Fermentation: Produce foods (yogurt, cheese), beverages (beer, wine), industrial chemicals
• Nigerian context: Local brewing, food processing rely on microbial fermentation
• Decomposition: Break down organic matter, recycle nutrients
• Nigerian context: Microbes used in bioremediation of oil-polluted Niger Delta
• Nigerian context: Infectious diseases are major causes of morbidity and mortality
• Nigerian context: Animal diseases reduce productivity and food security
• Nigerian context: Crop diseases cause significant yield losses
• Microbial growth causes spoilage, foodborne illness
• Nigerian context: Post-harvest losses due to microbial spoilage
a) Nutrients: Carbon, nitrogen, minerals, vitamins
b) Temperature: Optimal ranges for different microbes
c) pH: Acidic, neutral, or alkaline preferences
d) Oxygen: Aerobic, anaerobic, facultative requirements
e) Water activity: Moisture required for growth
a) Lag phase: Adaptation to new environment
c) Stationary phase: Growth rate equals death rate
d) Death phase: Decline in viable cells
• Nigerian Context: Understanding growth conditions helps control pathogens and optimize beneficial microbes
Control and Prevention of Microorganism Diseases (Public Health):
• Nigerian context: Handwashing campaigns reduce diarrheal diseases
• Nigerian context: Improved sanitation reduces disease transmission
• Environmental management to reduce breeding sites
• Nigerian context: ITNs are key malaria control tool
• Immunization against viral and bacterial diseases
• Nigerian context: National immunization programs protect children
• Nigerian context: Antimicrobial resistance is a growing concern
• Nigerian context: Traditional preservation methods reduce foodborne illness
• Divide students into working groups, provide each group with sterilized Petri-dishes which contain culture medium
• Students in each group grow cultures of microorganisms from air, water, and under the fingernails
• Collect and identify common vectors (mosquitoes, flies)
• Discuss their role in disease transmission
• 'All microorganisms are harmful': Many are beneficial or essential
• 'Antibiotics work against viruses': Antibiotics only target bacteria
• 'Sterilization is always necessary': Many situations require only sanitation
• 'Traditional methods are always ineffective': Many traditional practices effectively control microbes
• Microorganisms impact health, agriculture, and environment in Nigeria
• Public health measures reduce microbial disease burden
• Beneficial microbes support traditional foods and sustainable agriculture
• Research and education are key to harnessing microbial benefits and controlling harms
• Microorganisms are diverse, ubiquitous, and impactful
• They have both beneficial and harmful effects on humans, agriculture, and environment
• Understanding their biology enables effective control of pathogens and utilization of beneficial microbes
• In Nigeria, microbial knowledge supports public health, food security, and sustainable development
diagramDescriptions: Diagram showing classification of microorganisms: bacteria, viruses, fungi, protozoa, algae
Illustration of vector-borne disease transmission cycle
Microbial growth curve with phases labeled
Public health interventions pyramid: hygiene, sanitation, vector control, vaccination
Nigerian examples: Mosquitoes and malaria, fermented foods, bioremediation in Niger Delta
subUnits.youtubeQuery: Microorganisms classification beneficial harmful biology SSS1 site:youtube.com

quiz.q: Which of the following is a prokaryotic microorganism?
quiz.explanation: Bacteria are prokaryotic (lack a nucleus); fungi, protozoa, and algae are eukaryotic.

quiz.q: A biological vector for malaria is:
quiz.explanation: Mosquitoes (Anopheles species) are biological vectors for malaria; the Plasmodium parasite develops within the mosquito.

quiz.q: A beneficial effect of microorganisms in agriculture is:
quiz.explanation: Rhizobium bacteria fix atmospheric nitrogen in legume root nodules, improving soil fertility naturally.

quiz.q: The phase of microbial growth with the fastest increase in cell number is:
quiz.explanation: The log (exponential) phase is when cells divide at their maximum rate, leading to rapid population increase.

quiz.q: A traditional Nigerian fermented food that uses beneficial microbes is:
quiz.explanation: Ogi is a fermented cereal product; fermentation by beneficial microbes improves nutrition and preservation.
Antibiotics target bacterial structures or processes; they are not effective against viruses, fungi, or protozoa.

quiz.q: A public health measure to control malaria in Nigeria is:
quiz.explanation: Insecticide-treated bed nets (ITNs) are a key malaria control intervention in Nigeria, reducing mosquito bites.

quiz.q: Understanding microorganisms is important for Nigerian agriculture because it:
opts: Helps manage soil fertility, control diseases, and improve food safety
quiz.explanation: Knowledge of microbes helps farmers enhance soil fertility, manage crop/livestock diseases, and ensure food safety.
Bioremediation uses microbes to break down pollutants like oil, pesticides, and industrial chemicals, cleaning contaminated environments.

quiz.q: A common misconception about microorganisms is:
opts: Microbes are essential for nutrient cycling
quiz.explanation: While some microorganisms cause diseases, many are beneficial or essential for ecosystems, human health, and industry.
